title: Contracts – How to Protect Yourself Legally
description: Why well-drafted contracts are essential in business and everyday life. What makes a contract valid, common pitfalls to avoid, and when to use a notary.
content:
Why Contracts Matter

A contract is the foundation of every business relationship and many private arrangements. A well-drafted contract protects the interests of both parties, defines their rights and obligations clearly, and minimises the risk of disputes. The absence of a contract or an insufficiently detailed one is one of the most common sources of legal conflicts – both between businesses and in private relations.

Specter & Associates Law Firm in Warsaw specialises in drafting, reviewing, and negotiating contracts across all areas of civil and commercial law.

What Makes a Contract Valid?

Under Polish civil law (the Civil Code), a contract must meet several requirements to be valid:

  • Legal capacity – both parties must have the capacity to enter into contracts. Adults have full capacity; minors and incapacitated persons require the consent of a legal representative.
  • Genuine declarations of intent – the contract must reflect the true intentions of both parties, free from mistake, duress, or fraud.
  • Lawful purpose and content – a contract may not violate the law, social norms, or be intended to circumvent legal provisions.
  • Form of the contract – most contracts may be entered into in any form (including orally), but the law requires written, notarial, or electronic form for certain types of agreement.

Common Pitfalls in Contracts

Many disputes arise from drafting errors that can easily be avoided with legal assistance:

  • Vague description of the performance – unclear description of goods, services, or amounts leads to divergent interpretations.
  • No penalty clauses – without sanctions for non-performance, the other party may have no incentive to comply with deadlines.
  • No governing law or jurisdiction clauses – particularly important in cross-border contracts.
  • No force majeure provisions – without a force majeure clause, a party may face liability in unforeseeable circumstances.
  • Non-compliance with applicable regulations – for example, omitting GDPR requirements in data processing agreements.

When Is Notarial Form Required?

Polish law requires notarial deed form for particularly significant transactions, including:

  • sale and purchase of real estate,
  • establishing a mortgage,
  • articles of association of a limited liability company,
  • deeds of gift of real estate,
  • notarial wills,
  • agreements on separation of marital property.

Failure to comply with the notarial form requirement results in the nullity of the contract.

How to Ensure Legal Protection Before Signing

Before signing any significant contract, it is advisable to:

  1. Read the entire document carefully – do not skip any clauses.
  2. Verify the counterparty's credibility (company register, tax ID, history).
  3. Have the contract reviewed by a lawyer.
  4. Negotiate unfavourable provisions before signing, not after.
  5. Keep the original contract in a safe place.

Specter & Associates Law Firm offers comprehensive contract review and legal opinions. Before you sign – contact us. We protect your interests at every stage of the transaction.
